metadata.description: A curated knowledge base on cyber hygiene practices, threats, and source-grounded recommendations. The knowledge base supports a knowledge graph-grounded conversational agent and enables evaluation across validity, traceability, and stability.
curation_notes: Some threat mappings are initially inferred from Karayel et al.'s cyber hygiene categories and should later be supported by additional sources.
Page paragraph numbers are counted visually from the top-left of the page to the bottom-right of the page, regardless of section boundaries.

sources.title: Human factors in remote work: examining cyber hygiene practices
sources.notes: Primary source for cyber hygiene practices among remote workers and for the five cyber hygiene categories used as the initial taxonomy of the knowledge base.

sources.title: Cyber hygiene: The concept, its measure, and its initial tests
sources.notes: Original source for the Cyber Hygiene Inventory and its five cyber hygiene dimensions.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: General cyber hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: General concepts and foundational definitions related to cyber hygiene.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Storage and device hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Security measures and behaviors related to the configuration settings of digital devices.
examples: Conducting virus scans on storage devices
Keeping virus protection and device software up to date

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Transmission hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors when connecting to unknown or insecure wireless networks.
examples: Checking the quality of SSL certificates
Checking the lock icon in the web browser

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Social media hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors during the general use of social media.
examples: Evaluating the authenticity of friend requests
Being aware of who you are friends with on social media
Preventing location information from leaking in posts

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Authentication and credential hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors related to the use of passwords and authentication.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Email and messaging hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors related to email usage and messaging.
examples: Checking the subject of an incoming email
Checking the sender's email domain name
Checking spelling and grammar of email requests

threats.name: Malware infection
threats.definition: A compromise in which malicious software infects a device or storage medium.
threats.support_notes: Inferred from storage and device hygiene practices such as virus scans and keeping protection software up to date. Requires an additional source for a direct malware definition.

threats.name: Data loss
threats.definition: Loss of access to important files or information due to device failure, accidental deletion, malware, or other incidents.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. directly mention backing up important files and secure data storage, but additional sources may be used to strengthen the broader data loss definition.

threats.name: Ransomware
threats.definition: A form of malware that restricts access to data or systems until a ransom is paid.
threats.support_notes: Inferred from backup, malware, and email hygiene practices. Requires an additional source for a direct ransomware definition and mapping.

threats.name: Unpatched software
threats.definition: Risk arising from outdated software that may contain unresolved security vulnerabilities.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. directly mention keeping device software up to date, but an additional source should support the specific vulnerability-risk framing.

threats.name: Insecure connection
threats.definition: A network or website connection that does not provide sufficient protection for transmitted information.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. directly link transmission hygiene to unknown or insecure wireless networks and websites without SSL security certificates.

threats.name: Man-in-the-middle attack
threats.definition: An attack in which a third party intercepts or manipulates communication between two parties.
threats.support_notes: Inferred from SSL certificate and insecure connection practices. Requires an additional source for a direct man-in-the-middle definition.

threats.name: Social engineering
threats.definition: Manipulation of users into revealing information or performing unsafe actions.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. explicitly mention social engineering frauds in relation to email and messaging hygiene in remote work.

threats.name: Impersonation
threats.definition: A deception tactic in which an attacker pretends to be a trusted person, organization, or account.
threats.support_notes: Supported by practices such as evaluating authenticity of social media friend requests and checking sender email domains. Additional sources may strengthen the explicit impersonation framing.

threats.name: Oversharing personal information
threats.definition: Exposure of personal or sensitive information through excessive or poorly controlled sharing.
threats.support_notes: Supported by Karayel et al.'s social media hygiene examples, including awareness of who users are friends with and managing privacy settings.

threats.name: Privacy exposure
threats.definition: Risk of unintended disclosure of personal information due to weak privacy settings or unsafe online behavior.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. directly include managing privacy settings within social media hygiene.

threats.name: Credential targeting
threats.definition: Attempts by attackers to target user information, usernames, passwords, or authentication mechanisms.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. discuss authentication and credential hygiene and state that user information and passwords are frequently targeted by hackers.

threats.name: Credential theft
threats.definition: Unauthorized acquisition or misuse of usernames, passwords, or other authentication information.
threats.support_notes: Closely related to credential targeting, but the specific theft framing should be supported by an additional source.

threats.name: Brute-force credential attack
threats.definition: An attempt to guess credentials through repeated login attempts.
threats.support_notes: Inferred from complex password practices. Requires an additional source for a direct brute-force definition.

threats.name: Unauthorized access
threats.definition: Access to an account, device, or system without legitimate permission.
threats.support_notes: Inferred from authentication practices such as changing defaults and enabling MFA. Requires an additional source for a direct unauthorized-access definition.

threats.name: Phishing
threats.definition: A deceptive attack in which users are tricked into revealing information, clicking malicious links, opening harmful attachments, or performing unsafe actions.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. explicitly mention phishing attacks in relation to email and messaging hygiene.

threats.name: Email scam and e-fraud
threats.definition: A fraudulent email-based attempt to deceive users into unsafe actions or disclosure of information.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. mention e-fraud in relation to email and messaging hygiene.

threats.name: User noncompliance with security practices
threats.definition: A behavioral cybersecurity risk in which users fail to follow recommended or required security practices, increasing the likelihood of security incidents.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. discuss human factors as a weak link in security systems and state that noncompliance with security practices is a primary cause of security breaches.

threats.name: Insufficient organizational cybersecurity measures
threats.definition: A contextual cybersecurity risk in which weak or insufficient organizational security measures may be associated with poorer employee cyber hygiene practices.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. report a statistically significant positive relationship between organizational cybersecurity measures and employees' cyber hygiene practices.

practices.name: Conduct virus scans on storage devices
practices.description: Users should conduct virus scans on storage devices to detect potentially malicious or infected files.

practices.name: Back up important files
practices.description: Users should back up important files to reduce the impact of data loss, malware, or device failure.

practices.name: Keep protection software up to date
practices.description: Users should keep antivirus protection and device software up to date.

practices.name: Check SSL certificate quality
practices.description: Users should check whether a website uses a valid SSL certificate before entering sensitive information.

practices.name: Check browser lock icon
practices.description: Users should check for the browser lock icon to identify whether a connection appears secured.

practices.name: Evaluate authenticity of friend requests
practices.description: Users should evaluate whether social media friend requests are authentic before accepting them.

practices.name: Manage privacy settings
practices.description: Users should manage social media privacy settings to control who can view their information.

practices.name: Change default usernames and passwords
practices.description: Users should change default usernames and passwords to reduce the risk of unauthorized access.

practices.name: Create complex usernames and passwords
practices.description: Users should create complex usernames and passwords to reduce the risk of credential compromise.

practices.name: Enable multifactor authentication
practices.description: Users should enable multifactor authentication to add an additional layer of account security.

practices.name: Check incoming email subjects
practices.description: Users should inspect the subject of incoming emails for suspicious, unexpected, or urgency-based requests that may indicate phishing or e-fraud.

practices.name: Check sender email domain
practices.description: Users should check the sender's email domain name before trusting an email request, especially when the message asks for sensitive information or urgent action.

practices.name: Check incoming email headers
practices.description: Users should check an incoming email's header as part of email and messaging hygiene.

practices.name: Check spelling and grammar of email requests
practices.description: Users should inspect spelling and grammar in email requests as possible warning signs of suspicious, fraudulent, or socially engineered messages.

practices.name: Address individual user behavior
practices.description: Cybersecurity guidance should address individual user behavior in addition to technical security measures.

practices.name: Use strong passwords
practices.description: Users should use strong passwords as part of routine cyber hygiene behavior.

practices.name: Change passwords regularly
practices.description: Users should change passwords regularly as part of maintaining cyber hygiene.

practices.name: Avoid password reuse
practices.description: Users should avoid reusing passwords across accounts to reduce credential-related risk.

practices.name: Update antivirus software
practices.description: Users should keep antivirus software up to date to reduce exposure to cyber threats.

practices.name: Securely store online information
practices.description: Users should securely store online information to protect personal and sensitive data.

practices.name: Conduct appropriate security scans
practices.description: Users should conduct appropriate security scans to detect cyber threats or attack attempts.

practices.name: Strengthen organizational cybersecurity measures
practices.description: Organizations should strengthen cybersecurity measures that support employees' cyber hygiene practices.

practices.name: Provide cybersecurity training
practices.description: Organizations should educate and train employees on cyber hygiene to support improved cyber hygiene behavior.

practices.name: Provide tailored cybersecurity training
practices.description: Organizations should tailor cyber hygiene improvement initiatives and training to employees' characteristics, such as educational level, age, or comprehension needs.

practices.name: Develop cybersecurity policies and procedures
practices.description: Organizations should develop cybersecurity policies and procedures that support remote work systems and foster a strong cyber hygiene culture.

practices.name: Provide incident management training
practices.description: Organizations should adopt incident management training as part of advanced cybersecurity measures for coordinating and managing incidents.

practices.name: Use cybersecurity protocols for incident coordination
practices.description: Organizations should adopt cybersecurity protocols for coordinating and managing cybersecurity incidents.

practices.name: Use a VPN on free or public Wi-Fi
practices.description: Users should use a VPN when using free or public Wi-Fi.

practices.name: Store logins and passwords in an encrypted online password vault
practices.description: Users should store logins and passwords in encrypted online password vaults.

practices.name: Set online alerts for personal information
practices.description: Users should place online alerts for their name or personal information.

practices.name: Create new and unique logins and passwords
practices.description: Users should create new and unique logins and passwords for each account.

practices.name: Manage browser-stored passwords
practices.description: Users should manage passwords stored by browsers as part of authentication and credential hygiene.

practices.name: Know social media connections
practices.description: Users should be aware of who they are connected to on social media.

practices.name: Reassess social media friends and connections
practices.description: Users should periodically reassess their social media friends and connections.

practices.name: Prevent location leaks in social media posts
practices.description: Users should ensure that their location information is not leaked in social media posts.

practices.name: Enable firewalls
practices.description: Users should enable firewalls on computing devices as part of storage and device hygiene.

practices.name: Check device operating system and software updates
practices.description: Users should check whether their device has the latest operating system, software update, or patch.

practices.name: Monitor device resource and network usage
practices.description: Users should monitor device processes such as CPU, power, and network usage.

practices.name: Check email hyperlinks and attachments from unknown senders
practices.description: Users should check hyperlinks or attachments in emails from unknown senders as part of email and messaging hygiene.

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene as routine security behavior for protecting personal information
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene refers to routine cybersecurity practices that online users adopt to protect the security and integrity of their personal information on internet-connected devices against cyber threats.
knowledge_units.explanation: Cyber hygiene consists of regular security behaviors and habits that help protect internet-enabled devices and the safety and integrity of personal information from cyber threats.
recommended_actions: Adopt routine security habits when using internet-connected devices.
Protect personal, financial, and social information during online activity.
Follow security guidelines that reduce exposure to cyber threats.
Maintain secure behavior across devices, accounts, networks, and online communication.
question_templates: What is cyber hygiene?
Why is cyber hygiene important?
What does cyber hygiene mean?
What kinds of behaviors are included in cyber hygiene?
How does cyber hygiene reduce cybersecurity risk?

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Cyber hygiene refers to a set of cybersecurity practices that online users should adopt to safeguard the security and integrity of their personal information on internet-connected devices against potential cyber threats.
As a fundamental principle of information security, cyber hygiene comprises simple, routine measures that mitigate the risk of cyber threats.

the cyber security practices that online consumers should engage in to protect the safety and integrity of their personal information on their Internet enabled devices from being compromised in a cyber-attack

validity_facts.fact: Cyber hygiene refers to cybersecurity practices adopted by online users.
Cyber hygiene aims to protect the security and integrity of personal information.
Cyber hygiene applies to internet-connected devices.
Cyber hygiene consists of simple, routine measures that reduce cyber threat risk.
Vishwanath et al. define cyber hygiene as cybersecurity practices online consumers should engage in.
Cyber hygiene protects the safety and integrity of personal information on internet-enabled devices.
Cyber hygiene aims to prevent personal information from being compromised in a cyber-attack.

traceability.minimum_traceability_requirement: The answer should cite Karayel et al. 2024 or Vishwanath et al. 2020 and should be traceable to Karayel et al.'s Section 2.1, Cyber hygiene, or Vishwanath et al.'s Section 1, Defining cyber hygiene.

avoid_claims: Cyber hygiene is only about antivirus software
Cyber hygiene is only relevant to organizations
Cyber hygiene completely eliminates cybersecurity risk

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene guidance should address user behavior as well as technical controls
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene guidance should address individual user behavior, because technical cybersecurity measures alone are insufficient for effective cybersecurity.
knowledge_units.explanation: Karayel et al. emphasize that cybersecurity depends not only on organizational or technical measures, but also on individual behavior. Users who do not comply with security practices can weaken otherwise secure systems, making behavioral guidance an important part of cyber hygiene.
recommended_actions: Address user behavior when designing cyber hygiene guidance.
Encourage users to follow recommended security practices consistently.
Combine technical cybersecurity measures with awareness of individual security behavior.
Recognize that noncompliance with security practices can increase security risk.
question_templates: Why does cyber hygiene depend on user behavior?
Are technical cybersecurity measures enough to ensure good cyber hygiene?
Why should cyber hygiene guidance focus on people as well as technology?
How do human factors affect cybersecurity?
Why is user compliance important for cyber hygiene?

evidence.exact_supporting_text: cybersecurity investments and measures alone are insufficient
Human factors often constitute the weakest link
noncompliance with security practices

validity_facts.fact: Technical cybersecurity measures alone are insufficient for effective cybersecurity.
Cybersecurity also requires attention to individual user behavior.
Human factors can weaken security systems.
Noncompliance with security practices is an important cause of security breaches.

traceability.minimum_traceability_requirement: The answer should cite Karayel et al. 2024 and should be traceable to the Introduction section discussing technical measures, individual behavior, and human factors.

must_include: noncompliance with security practices increases risk
avoid_claims: Cybersecurity is only a technical problem
User behavior does not matter if technical controls are strong
Human error is the only cause of cybersecurity breaches

knowledge_units.title: Authentication hygiene includes changing defaults, creating complex credentials, and enabling MFA
knowledge_units.claim: Authentication and credential hygiene involves changing default usernames and passwords, creating complex or unique credentials, managing browser-stored passwords, and enabling multifactor authentication.
knowledge_units.explanation: These practices describe authentication and credential hygiene behaviors that help users manage access to accounts and systems through usernames, passwords, stored credentials, and authentication mechanisms. Karayel et al. support changing defaults, creating complex credentials, and enabling multifactor authentication, while Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory additionally includes creating new and unique logins and passwords and managing browser-stored passwords.
recommended_actions: Change default usernames and passwords.
Create complex usernames and passwords.
Create new and unique logins and passwords for each account.
Manage passwords stored by browsers.
Enable two-factor or multifactor authentication where possible.
question_templates: What are good authentication and credential hygiene practices?
How can users improve password and authentication security?
What should remote workers do to protect their login credentials?

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Changing default usernames and passwords, creating complex usernames and passwords and enabling multifactor authentication

High authentication and credential hygiene, which involve using complex usernames and passwords, changing defaults and enabling multifactor authentication, are observed in the IT, defense, automotive and manufacturing industries. In contrast, the food, service and construction industries demonstrate the lowest levels. As user information and passwords are frequently targeted by hackers, maintaining high cyber hygiene is crucial for all internet users.
Changing default username from administrator to something unique on all Internet enabled devices

knowledge_units.title: Routine cyber hygiene practices include password, antivirus, storage, and security scanning behaviors
knowledge_units.claim: Routine cyber hygiene practices include checking devices for cyber threats, using strong passwords, changing passwords regularly, avoiding password reuse, updating antivirus software, securely storing online information, and conducting security scans.
knowledge_units.explanation: These practices represent recurring security habits that help users protect personal information, devices, and accounts from cyber threats. They complement the five cyber hygiene categories by providing concrete examples of day-to-day protective behavior.
recommended_actions: Check computers and devices for cyber threats or attack attempts.
Use strong passwords.
Change passwords regularly where appropriate.
Avoid reusing passwords across accounts.
Keep antivirus software up to date.
Securely store online information.
Conduct appropriate security scans.
question_templates: What are examples of routine cyber hygiene practices?

Which routine behaviors help users reduce cybersecurity risk?
What basic cyber hygiene practices protect personal information?

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Examples include routinely checking computers for cyber threats or attack attempts, using strong passwords, changing passwords regularly and avoiding password reuse, updating antivirus software, securely storing online information and conducting appropriate security scans.

validity_facts.fact: Routine cyber hygiene includes checking computers for cyber threats or attack attempts.
Routine cyber hygiene includes using strong passwords.
Routine cyber hygiene includes changing passwords regularly.
Routine cyber hygiene includes avoiding password reuse.
Routine cyber hygiene includes updating antivirus software.
Routine cyber hygiene includes securely storing online information.
Routine cyber hygiene includes conducting appropriate security scans.

traceability.minimum_traceability_requirement: The answer should cite Karayel et al. 2024 and should be traceable to Section 2.1, Cyber hygiene, where routine cyber hygiene examples are listed.

avoid_claims: Only technical tools are needed for cyber hygiene
Antivirus software alone is enough for good cyber hygiene
Password reuse is safe if the password is strong
Cyber hygiene completely eliminates all cyber risk

knowledge_units.title: Email and messaging hygiene helps users detect phishing, e-fraud, and social engineering attempts
knowledge_units.claim: Email and messaging hygiene involves checking email subjects, incoming email headers, sender domains, hyperlinks or attachments from unknown senders, and the spelling and grammar of email requests. Karayel et al. link email and messaging hygiene to caution against phishing, e-fraud, and social engineering attempts.
knowledge_units.explanation: Remote workers rely heavily on email and messaging platforms for communication. Karayel et al. identify email and messaging hygiene as an important cyber hygiene subdimension for preventing phishing, e-fraud, and social engineering-related attacks. Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory further lists checking incoming email headers, sender email domain names, hyperlinks or attachments from unknown senders, and grammatical or typographical errors as email and messaging hygiene items.
recommended_actions: Check the subject of incoming emails for suspicious or unexpected requests.
Check the header of incoming emails as part of email and messaging hygiene.
Check the sender's email domain before trusting an email request.
Check hyperlinks or attachments in emails from unknown senders.
Inspect grammatical or typographical errors in email requests.
Remain cautious when receiving unexpected requests through email or messaging platforms.
question_templates: How can users reduce phishing risk through email hygiene?
What email and messaging hygiene practices help prevent phishing?
What should remote workers check in suspicious emails?
How does email hygiene help prevent social engineering attacks?

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Checking the subject of an incoming email, checking the email domain name of the sender and checking the spelling and grammar of email requests
E-mail and messaging hygiene is the highest subdimension for both sectors, requiring a high level of awareness to prevent cyberattacks like phishing and e-fraud
Maintaining caution and high awareness is vital for preventing social engineering frauds and phishing attacks
Checking an incoming email's header

knowledge_units.title: Transmission hygiene involves checking secure website connections and SSL indicators
knowledge_units.claim: Transmission hygiene involves checking SSL certificate quality and the browser lock icon to reduce risky behavior when using websites or networks that may not provide secure connections.
knowledge_units.explanation: Karayel et al. describe transmission hygiene as cyber hygiene behavior related to connections with unknown or insecure wireless networks. They also associate browsing and transmission hygiene with avoiding websites that lack SSL security certificates. Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory additionally includes other transmission and browsing hygiene practices, such as VPN use on free or public Wi-Fi, encrypted online password vaults, and online alerts for personal information.
recommended_actions: Check whether a website has a valid SSL certificate before entering sensitive information.
Check for the browser lock icon when assessing whether a website connection appears secured.
Be cautious when connecting to unknown or insecure wireless networks.
Avoid entering sensitive information on websites that lack SSL security indicators.

knowledge_units.title: Transmission hygiene also includes VPN use, encrypted password vaults, and personal-information alerts
knowledge_units.claim: Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory includes additional transmission and browsing hygiene practices such as using a VPN on free or public Wi-Fi, storing logins and passwords in encrypted online password vaults, and placing online alerts for one's name or personal information.
knowledge_units.explanation: In the Cyber Hygiene Inventory, Vishwanath et al. include several practices under transmission and browsing hygiene beyond checking SSL indicators. These include VPN use on free or public Wi-Fi, encrypted online password vault use, and online alerts for personal information. These items should be treated as source-listed CHI practices, while their detailed threat mappings may require additional sources.
recommended_actions: Use a VPN when using free or public Wi-Fi.
Store logins and passwords in encrypted online password vaults.
Place online alerts for your name or personal information.

knowledge_units.title: Storage and device hygiene includes backups, antivirus updates, secure storage, and virus scans
knowledge_units.claim: Storage and device hygiene involves conducting virus scans, backing up important files, keeping virus protection and device software up to date, enabling firewalls, checking device updates, monitoring device usage, and securely storing data.
knowledge_units.explanation: Karayel et al. describe storage and device hygiene as cyber hygiene behavior related to the configuration and security of digital devices. This includes scanning devices for viruses, backing up important files, keeping protection software updated, and securely storing data. Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory additionally includes enabling firewalls, checking for operating system or software updates, keeping virus protection updated, and monitoring CPU, power, or network usage.
recommended_actions: Conduct virus scans or security scans on storage devices.
Back up important files.
Keep antivirus, virus protection, and device software up to date.
Check whether devices have the latest operating system, software update, or patch.
Enable firewalls on computing devices.
Monitor device processes such as CPU, power, and network usage.
Securely store data, including physical and cloud-based information.

knowledge_units.title: Organizational cybersecurity measures are associated with employee cyber hygiene practices
knowledge_units.claim: Stronger organizational cybersecurity measures are associated with improved cyber hygiene practices among remote employees.
knowledge_units.explanation: Karayel et al. found a statistically significant positive relationship between the cybersecurity measures adopted by organizations and employees' cyber hygiene practices. This suggests that individual cyber hygiene should be understood not only as a personal behavior issue, but also as something influenced by the organizational security environment.
recommended_actions: Strengthen organizational cybersecurity measures that support employees' cyber hygiene practices.
Consider organizational cybersecurity measures when trying to improve employees' cyber hygiene.
Treat employee cyber hygiene as connected to the broader organizational security environment.

knowledge_units.explanation: Karayel et al. recommend organizational support for cyber hygiene in remote work contexts, including stronger cybersecurity measures, employee education and training, tailored improvement initiatives, cybersecurity policies, and tools or protocols for managing incidents.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: To mitigate this risk and improve employees’ cyber hygiene, it is essential to strengthen cyber security measures within the public sector’s remote work systems. In addition, it is advisable to educate and train employees on cyber hygiene to cultivate a cyber security culture within organizations. Developing cyber security policies for remote work systems is recommended for both public and private institutions to enhance cyber hygiene.

Developing comprehensive training programs, raising awareness and implementing effective policies and procedures can contribute to fostering a culture of strong cyber hygiene within organizations. This will help minimize potential risks associated with remote work and ensure the security of digital assets.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: To mitigate the risks posed by cyberattacks, organizations and decision-makers should invest in advanced cybersecurity measures. We recommend adopting new-generation tools and techniques, such as incident management training, educational booklets and cybersecurity protocols for coordinating and managing incidents. These efforts will help improve employees’ cyber hygiene behaviors.

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene is a multidimensional and context-adaptable construct
knowledge_units.claim: The Cyber Hygiene Inventory organizes user cyber hygiene into five dimensions: storage/device, transmission/browsing, social media, authentication/credential, and email/messaging hygiene. Vishwanath et al. empirically identify these dimensions, and Karayel et al. also applies comparable cyber hygiene categories in the remote-work context.
knowledge_units.explanation: Vishwanath et al. conceptualize cyber hygiene as an overarching construct whose subdimensions are not fixed in advance, but can be empirically identified and adapted to context. This supports representing cyber hygiene as a set of categories rather than as a single undifferentiated list of security advice.
recommended_actions: Treat cyber hygiene as a multidimensional concept rather than a single behavior.
Organize cyber hygiene guidance into specific categories or subdimensions.
Adapt cyber hygiene guidance to the user's context, role, technology, and organizational environment.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: this definition of cyber hygiene provides a first-order conceptualization–an overarching theoretical definition that does not specify or constrain the constituent factors that form the sub dimensions of cyber hygiene
evidence.paragraph: continues from bottom of page 2 to top of page 3

evidence.exact_supporting_text: the cyber hygiene definition and guidelines can be broad enough to apply to the general consumers yet moldable to specific professional or cultural contexts
the subdimensions that comprise cyber hygiene for employees would then be determined by the role of the employee, the type of the organization, or the sector within which the organization operates
the conceptual definition accommodates a wide range of users, technologies, related practices, and changes overtime

validity_facts.fact: Cyber hygiene is conceptualized as an overarching construct whose subdimensions are not fixed in advance.
The subdimensions of cyber hygiene can be determined empirically.
Cyber hygiene guidance can be adapted to professional or cultural contexts.

The cyber hygiene definition can accommodate different users, technologies, practices, and technological changes over time.

knowledge_units.title: The Cyber Hygiene Inventory identifies five dimensions of user cyber hygiene
knowledge_units.claim: The Cyber Hygiene Inventory organizes user cyber hygiene into five empirically identified dimensions: storage and device hygiene, transmission and browsing hygiene, social media hygiene, authentication and credential hygiene, and email and messaging hygiene.
knowledge_units.explanation: Vishwanath et al. developed the Cyber Hygiene Inventory through concept mapping, expert sorting, cluster analysis, and confirmatory factor analysis. The resulting inventory identifies five dimensions of cyber hygiene, which support the category structure used in this knowledge base.
recommended_actions: Use the five Cyber Hygiene Inventory dimensions to organize cyber hygiene guidance.
Classify cyber hygiene practices under storage/device, transmission, social media, authentication/credential, and email/messaging hygiene.
Use these dimensions to structure benchmark questions and agent responses.
question_templates: What are the five dimensions of cyber hygiene?

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Using this process, the research team settled on dimension names that conveniently fit the acronym: SAFETY, where S: storage and device hygiene; A: authentication and credential hygiene; F: Facebook and social media hygiene; E: email and messaging hygiene, T: transmission and browsing hygiene.
The final 18 items, encompassing five dimensions of cyber hygiene, measure users' cognitive associations between different online actions and the extent to which the individual is aware of them.

The research defined cyber hygiene as a second-order hierarchical construct and empirically found five sub-dimensions or latent factors of cyber hygiene, each covering a specific facet of online behavior.

The five cyber hygiene dimensions were empirically identified as latent factors.
Each cyber hygiene dimension covers a specific facet of online behavior.

knowledge_units.title: Social media hygiene includes checking request authenticity, managing privacy, reviewing connections, and preventing location leaks
knowledge_units.claim: Social media hygiene involves assessing the authenticity of social media requests, knowing and reassessing one's social media connections, managing privacy settings, and ensuring that location information is not leaked in posts.
knowledge_units.explanation: Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory includes several social media hygiene practices related to social media connections, privacy settings, request authenticity, and location disclosure. These practices support safer social media behavior by helping users control who can access their information and by reducing unintended exposure of personal or location information.
recommended_actions: Assess the authenticity of social media friend or information requests before responding.
Be aware of who you are connected to on social media.
Reassess your social media friends and connections periodically.
Manage privacy settings on social media platforms.
Ensure that location information is not leaked in social media posts.

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene awareness is associated with greater enactment of cyber hygiene behaviors
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene awareness is associated with greater enactment of cyber hygiene behaviors across the Cyber Hygiene Inventory dimensions.
knowledge_units.explanation: Vishwanath et al. report that users with high enactment of cyber hygiene behaviors also showed significantly higher awareness than users with low enactment across each Cyber Hygiene Inventory dimension. This supports the idea that cyber hygiene guidance should not only list practices, but also help users recognize and understand those practices.
recommended_actions: Increase users' awareness of cyber hygiene practices.
Help users understand why specific cyber hygiene behaviors matter.
Use cyber hygiene guidance to support both awareness and behavior enactment.
Treat awareness as an important part of improving cyber hygiene behavior.

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene is related to cyber safety-related beliefs, information processing, and online behavior
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene matters because it is significantly related to cyber safety-related self-beliefs about technology, cognitive information processing, and online safety-related behavior.
knowledge_units.explanation: Vishwanath et al. report that the Cyber Hygiene Inventory significantly predicts multiple cyber safety-related aspects of human cyber interaction, including technology-related self-beliefs, systematic processing of online information, and online behavior. This supports treating cyber hygiene as more than a checklist of practices: it is linked to how users think, evaluate information, and behave online.
recommended_actions: Treat cyber hygiene as relevant to users' beliefs, information processing, and online behavior.
Use cyber hygiene guidance to support safer online decision-making.
Explain why cyber hygiene practices matter instead of only listing what users should do.
Consider cyber hygiene behavior when evaluating cyber safety-related user behavior.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: cyber hygiene significantly predicts aspects of human cyber interaction that are pivotal to cyber safety including user self-beliefs about technology, how they cognitively process information online, and their online banking behavior

To this end, the survey focused on three different constructs related to cyber hygiene, each tapping into a pivotal facet of the human-cyber interaction process: a technology related user belief, a cognitive processing factor, and an online behavior that is of particular importance to cyber security research.

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene guidance needs clear conceptual boundaries
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene guidance needs clear conceptual boundaries because broad, inconsistent, or conflicting advice can confuse users and weaken their ability to protect themselves and the wider cyber environment.
knowledge_units.explanation: Vishwanath et al. argue that cyber hygiene is widely discussed but conceptually unclear, with advice ranging from obvious recommendations to highly specific technical guidance. This lack of clarity motivates the need for structured, evidence-grounded cyber hygiene guidance rather than unorganized security advice.
recommended_actions: Provide cyber hygiene guidance with clear conceptual boundaries.
Organize cyber hygiene advice into defined categories or dimensions.
Avoid giving users broad or conflicting advice without explaining its basis.
Use source-grounded guidance to reduce confusion about cyber hygiene practices.
question_templates: Why does cyber hygiene guidance need to be clear?
Why is inconsistent cyber hygiene advice a problem?
Why should cyber hygiene guidance be structured?
Why does Neo use a knowledge base for cyber hygiene advice?
How does unclear cyber hygiene advice affect users?

evidence.exact_supporting_text: But while many experts lament the lack of cyber hygiene, it's unclear what cyber hygiene really means. In fact, there isn't a single academic research article that explicates the construct or presents its measurement.
there exists no scholarly research explicating the concept or its measurement

Having such an important concept be so vaguely defined, broadly construed, and, for the most part, untested, does little to improve cyber resilience. All it does is confuse IT managers and end-users by providing conflicting advice that further weakens our ability to protect the cyber space.

validity_facts.fact: Vishwanath et al. state that the meaning of cyber hygiene has been unclear.
Vishwanath et al. state that prior scholarly research had not explicated the concept or measurement of cyber hygiene.
Unclear or inconsistent cyber hygiene advice can motivate the need for structured and conceptually grounded cyber hygiene guidance.

traceability.minimum_traceability_requirement: The answer should cite Vishwanath et al. 2020 and should be traceable to the Introduction section, where the paper discusses conceptual uncertainty around cyber hygiene, the lack of prior scholarly explication and measurement, and the problem that vague or conflicting advice can confuse users and weaken cyber resilience.

avoid_claims: All existing cyber hygiene advice is wrong

Neo solves all cyber hygiene confusion
Vishwanath et al. directly evaluate LLM-based cyber hygiene assistants
